definition: Sell side liquidity (SSL) is the accumulation of stop-loss orders from long traders and sell-stop orders located below swing lows, equal lows, range lows, or support levels. Smart money targets these pools to pair orders, often sweeping them before a reversal or continuation. A sweep is confirmed when price breaks below the level, triggers stops, and quickly reverses with displacement and a market structure shift.
identification_rules: Identify swing lows (low with a higher low on each side) or equal lows (two or more lows at roughly the same price) on the relevant time frame. Mark the area below these lows as sell side liquidity.
Also consider previous day low, previous week low, session lows (Asia, London, New York), and range lows (using candle bodies) as sell side liquidity pools.
For bearish targets, confirm a bearish context: price reacting from a higher-timeframe PD array (order block, FVG) with displacement lower, or a change in state of delivery (CISD).
To confirm a sweep, observe price trading below the identified low, ideally with a wick and a close back inside the prior range, or a clear take-out during expansion.
For reversal setups, a valid sweep must be followed by aggressive displacement and a market structure shift to the upside (e.g., price closing on the higher side, breaking structure).
Ensure the swept level is associated with a higher time frame important level (HTF FVG, prior order block, key swing low) for higher probability.
In the box setup, after an aggressive deviation above the range and an aggressive close back inside, the range low becomes the projected sell side liquidity target for shorts.
Combine with standard deviation projection to confirm -2 to -2.5 standard deviation levels aligning with the liquidity zone.
Check for confluence with other PD arrays (Order Block, Fair Value Gap) to enhance confirmation.
When price is in a consolidation range, treat the range low (using candle bodies, not wicks) as the sell side liquidity target.
In a bearish OTE scenario, drag Fibonacci from swing high to swing low; the stop-loss cluster below the swing low is the target sell side liquidity.
After a sweep, update the marked level to the next valid swing low if price continues lower.
trading_implication: Sell side liquidity serves as a downside draw and profit target for shorts, especially after a bearish change in state of delivery or rejection from a PD array, with entries taken at premium levels targeting SSL. A sweep of SSL followed by aggressive displacement higher validates long entry setups at discount levels (breaker blocks, order blocks, FVGs) with stops below the swept low. In range setups, SSL is targeted after aggressive deviations back into the range. It is fractal across timeframes, requiring minimum 2R targets.
common_mistakes: Treating a minor wick below a low as a valid sweep without waiting for a close back inside the range or aggressive displacement.
Entering long on a sweep without confirmation of aggressive displacement and a market structure shift to the upside.
Assuming every SSL sweep guarantees a reversal, ignoring that losses can still occur.
Targeting sell-side liquidity for shorts without first confirming a bearish change in state of delivery or bearish rejection from a PD array.
Treating any low sweep as SSL without associating it with a higher time frame important level (HTF FVG, prior order block, key swing low).
Marking wicks rather than candle bodies when defining swing lows / sell-side liquidity levels, or misidentifying swing lows by not confirming higher lows on both sides.
Overlooking equal lows and focusing only on single swing lows.
Ignoring higher time frame liquidity levels like previous day, week, or session lows.
Entering shorts toward SSL when the available reward is below 2R without projecting to further targets.
Looking for short setups in a range when the move back inside is not aggressive enough.
Placing stop-losses too tight above the swept low instead of beyond the swing low.
Expecting a move to sell-side liquidity without a confirmed bearish structural shift (e.g., an intermediate-term high).
Assuming price will retest a broken high instead of delivering to sell-side liquidity.
Trading solely based on the presence of sell-side liquidity without establishing bullish market structure first.
Assuming that price will immediately sweep sell-side liquidity without waiting for a clear manipulation and initiation sequence.
Entering a long trade before the sellside liquidity sweep occurs, leading to being stopped out.
Interpreting a sweep as a genuine breakdown and going short, missing the subsequent bullish reversal.
Trading sweeps in ranging or choppy markets where false signals are frequent.
Treating an unswept demand zone as valid, only to have price use it as liquidity instead.
Overlooking sellside liquidity formed by trendlines or break-of-structure points.
Sell Side Liquidity swept but not updating the marked level to the next valid swing low.
Ignoring equal lows and treating them as separate levels instead of merging.
Only marking swing lows on a single timeframe, ignoring multi-layered SSL (previous day low, session low, etc.).
Forcing a directional bias when price is in consolidation (indecision candles).
Entering long immediately after a sweep without waiting for price to displace through a Breaker Block or FVG.
Assuming a sweep guarantees a reversal; price may continue lower if it fails to aggressively return into the range.
Entering on a touch of the low without waiting for a close back inside the range to confirm the sweep.
Trading a sweep at an arbitrary low without higher timeframe important level support.
Relying solely on SMT divergence or SSL sweep without considering higher timeframe bias and structure.

related_concepts.term: Change in the state of delivery

merge_notes: School conflict: 4 ICT vs 1 SMC; picked ICT by majority. One SMC card defined SSL as also containing sell-stop orders from short traders with take-profits, which is a nuance not present in ICT cards; this was included in the definition. The Foundation card included specific candle types (directional/indecision) for identification; these were preserved. All identification rules from all cards were unioned and deduplicated.
